title: US Interceptor Crisis Deepens, Ukraine Seeks Missile Production License, South Korea's $37B Defense Breakout
subhead: The Iran war burned through American air defense munitions, exposing a cost asymmetry that favors cheap drones. Ukraine is now asking the US for a license to build Patriot interceptors domestically. South Korea's defense exports are on track to hit $37 billion this year. And Poland is steering EU defense loans into its own arms industry.

sections.heading: US Missile Stockpiles Running Bare After Iran War
sections.body:
A detailed analysis by Reason confirms what Pentagon officials have been dancing around for weeks: the US-Iran war burned through American air defense interceptor stocks at an unsustainable rate. Acting Navy Secretary Hung Cao told Congress in May that the US was 'doing a pause' on weapons sales to Taiwan 'in order to make sure we have the munitions we need' for the Iran conflict. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth walked that back days later, insisting he felt 'good about where we are' on munitions — but the gap between official reassurance and operational reality is widening.

Israel was rationing its high-end interceptors as the war progressed, with stocks falling to 'double digits,' according to a US source cited by Drop Site News. Iran was achieving an increasing hit rate with smaller barrages late in the conflict precisely because interceptors had been depleted. The RUSI think tank documented that the US and its partners used over 11,000 munitions in 16 days of the Iran war.

The cost asymmetry is stark: the US fires million-dollar interceptors at drones that cost a few thousand dollars to produce. As Justin Logan of the Cato Institute notes, 'the Americans like to insulate ourselves and our friends from adversaries' ability to retaliate, but that's extremely costly.' Future US wars may look 'more like Ukraine,' with heavy bombing on both sides, because the shield of air dominance that the US has enjoyed for decades is becoming unaffordable.
sections.whyItMatters: The interceptor shortage is not just a logistics problem — it reshapes deterrence. If the US cannot sustain the volume of air defense fire needed to protect allies and itself, the calculus of future conflicts changes fundamentally. Taiwan is already waiting on a $14 billion arms package that largely consists of Patriot ammunition and air defense weapons.

sections.heading: Ukraine Asks US for Domestic Anti-Ballistic Missile Production License
sections.body:
President Volodymyr Zelensky said at the G7 summit in France that Ukraine is seeking US approval to manufacture anti-ballistic missile systems and interceptors domestically, marking a significant shift in how Kyiv wants to address its air defense crisis. Zelensky said he raised the issue directly with President Trump, who responded 'positively' to the proposal.

The request comes as Ukraine faces a dire shortage of Patriot interceptors. After a Russian air raid killed 22 people in early June, Zelensky pleaded with European allies to speed up Patriot deliveries, saying the issue was 'no longer about financing' — there simply are not enough interceptors to go around. Ukraine even proposed 'borrowing' Patriot missiles from Germany, emptying German warehouses in exchange for an IOU.

Granting a production license would be unprecedented. The Biden administration had never approved such a step. The Eurasian Times reports that Ukraine is citing low Western production rates and the risk of exhausting existing stockpiles as justification. Zelensky emphasized that 'production isn't as high as our needs' and that manufacturing currently 'takes place in the US.'
sections.whyItMatters: If approved, this would be the first time the US licenses Patriot interceptor production to a foreign country — and to an active war zone. It signals that the conventional model of shipping finite stocks of interceptors may be giving way to distributed production, driven by the sheer mathematics of missile defense consumption.

sections.heading: South Korea's Defense Exports on Track for $37 Billion
sections.body:
South Korea's defense industry is experiencing a structural breakout. The country's four largest defense firms — Hanwha Aerospace, Hyundai Rotem, Korea Aerospace Industries (KAI), and LIG Nex1 — posted combined revenue of roughly 40.9 trillion won (~$30 billion) in 2025, up 81.6% year-over-year. For 2026, analysts project the figure will climb above 50.58 trillion won (~$37.4 billion), according to DB Investment & Securities.

At a seminar marking the 20th anniversary of its Defense Acquisition Program Administration, South Korean officials formally declared that defense exports are no longer a supplement to domestic procurement — they are 'a central growth engine driving the Republic of Korea as a whole,' per DAPA Minister Lee Yong-cheol. South Korea's annual defense exports grew from about $250 million when DAPA was founded to $15.4 billion last year.

Research presented at the seminar — analyzing data from roughly 2,000 defense companies — found that exports increased factory utilization, encouraged R&D investment, and eventually reduced manufacturing costs. Hyundai Rotem executive Kim Myung-keun said the large Polish K2 tank order achieved economies of scale, 'lowered costs, reduced the acquisition cost for our own military, and accelerated the development of upgraded models.' A $3.2 billion Cheongung-II missile defense deal with Saudi Arabia signed in late 2025 anchors a growing Middle East pipeline.
sections.whyItMatters: South Korea is crossing the threshold from emerging exporter to established global defense supplier. With the US stretching its own stocks thin and European countries scrambling to rearm, Seoul is positioning itself as the reliable alternative — competitive pricing, fast delivery, and now growing political alignment with NATO states. The $37 billion projection for 2026 puts it in direct competition with Germany for global top-ten defense revenues.

sections.heading: US Marines Plan Permanent Weapons Stockpile in Australia
sections.body:
US Navy tender documents published this month reveal advanced planning for a permanent war-ready weapons stockpile for the Marine Corps on Australia's southeast coast, in the state of Victoria. The facility, with $30 million allocated for warehouses and offices, will store 'crew-served weapons' and is expected to reach full capacity by 2028.

The stockpile will initially be housed in Melbourne before moving to US warehouses at the Australian military base at Bandiana in rural Victoria. The US Navy is seeking a global defense contractor to employ around 110 engineers, mechanics, and material specialists to manage it. A Lowy Institute report this week warned that China has the capability to strike northern Australia with ballistic missiles from its South China Sea outposts, likely a 'relevant consideration' in placing the stockpile in the southeast.

The Pentagon has asked Congress for $500 million next year to improve prepositioning of equipment and fuel across the Indo-Pacific to deter China. The first land stockpile in the Asia-Pacific is expected to open this year in the Philippines, close to potential South China Sea flashpoints.

Australian National University professor John Blaxland noted that the facility is 'a major change to Australian policy that ties Australia much more closely to America's strategic objectives in the region.' Australia does not permit foreign military bases on its soil, so the arrangement is structured as prepositioned equipment rather than a base.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first permanent Marine Corps weapons stockpile in Australia and the second land prepositioning site in the Indo-Pacific. It reflects a shift from symbolic rotational deployments to actual warfighting logistics — munitions and equipment positioned within range of potential conflict but far enough from Chinese missiles to survive a first strike.

sections.heading: Poland Channels EU Defense Loans Into Domestic Arms Industry
sections.body:
Newly appointed Polish Deputy Defence Minister Magdalena Sobkowiak-Czarnecka said at the opening of the Warsaw Defence Fair that Poland aims to steer as many EU countries as possible toward spending their share of EU defense loans on Polish-made weapons. Eight countries have already made concrete commitments, with more expected.

The EU's SAFE programme offers Poland around EUR 43.7 billion in low-interest loans for defense investment. By the end of May, Poland had signed the first round of contracts worth roughly PLN 120 billion (~EUR 28 billion) for combat vehicles, artillery, and other equipment from Polish defense firms — 62 contracts in total over three days.

Defence Minister Władysław Kosiniak-Kamysz said the government now plans to expand exports of Polish equipment, predicting that most European armies would buy Polish Piorun anti-aircraft missile systems while Baltic neighbours would purchase Baobab mine-laying vehicles.
sections.whyItMatters: Poland is not just buying weapons — it is building a domestic defense industrial base and trying to make itself the manufacturing hub for European rearmament. If it succeeds, it becomes a net exporter of defense equipment to EU partners, funded by EU loans. This would shift the center of European defense manufacturing eastward.

sections.heading: India's Defense Output Hits Record, Russia Wants BrahMos
sections.body:
India's defense production hit a record Rs 1.78 lakh crore (~$21 billion) in FY 2025-26, up 15.6% over the previous year and nearly a 110% increase in five years, according to Defence Ministry data. Private firms now account for nearly a quarter of total output, up steadily from previous years. Exports reached Rs 38,424 crore this year, with over 80 countries receiving Indian defense equipment.

In a striking development, BrahMos Aerospace revealed that Russia is keen to induct the BrahMos supersonic cruise missile into its own armed forces — and that India will manufacture these systems for Russia. Despite being a joint Indo-Russian venture for three decades, Russia had never previously inducted BrahMos. Its interest reportedly peaked after India announced export deals for BrahMos with countries including the Philippines.

Separately, India's Navy is commissioning three warships simultaneously on June 21 — INS Dunagiri (BrahMos-equipped frigate), INS Agray (shallow-water corvette), and INS Sanshodhak (survey ship) — all built domestically at Garden Reach Shipbuilders in Kolkata. India is aiming for full self-reliance in naval shipbuilding by 2047, with approximately 60 warships under various stages of development.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia seeking to buy BrahMos from India represents a reversal of the traditional Indo-Russian defense relationship, where India was always the buyer. It signals both the maturation of India's defense manufacturing and the strain on Russia's own production capacity after years of war. India is also pushing to become a net global defense exporter, not just a substitute supplier.

sections.heading: Ukrainian Drone Makers Pitch Combat-Proven Systems to Asian Militaries
sections.body:
Reuters reports that Ukrainian drone manufacturers are actively courting Asian military buyers — particularly Japan and Taiwan — as rising China-Taiwan tensions drive demand for combat-proven unmanned systems. Ukrainian firms are leveraging the unique advantage of having tested their products in full-scale warfare against a peer adversary.

The export push comes alongside a broader shift in how defense technology is delivered. Reuters Breakingviews reports on the rise of 'Defence-as-a-Service' in Ukraine, where private firms have shot down 20 Russian drones using contractor-operated systems. The model mirrors SaaS: instead of buying expensive hardware, clients rent defensive capability. It offers faster technology cycles but raises questions about accountability and the privatization of warfare.

Ukraine has also begun targeting Russian energy infrastructure at record distances. Zelensky noted at the G7 that Ukrainian long-range drones are causing 'serious problems' for Russia, with Tatneft, one of Russia's largest oil companies, introducing temporary fuel purchase limits at gas stations following sustained Ukrainian attacks on its refining capacity.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's drone industry is becoming an export force precisely because its products are combat-proven — a selling point no other drone exporter can currently match at scale. If Japan and Taiwan adopt Ukrainian drone technology, it creates a new defense supply chain that bypasses traditional Western contractors entirely.

bottomLine: The global defense industry is being reshaped by two simultaneous pressures: the American interceptor shortage exposed by the Iran war, and the scramble by US allies to build their own production capacity or find alternative suppliers. Ukraine's request for a Patriot production license, South Korea's $37 billion export surge, Poland's attempt to become the factory floor of European rearmament, and India's reversal of the Indo-Russian arms relationship all point in the same direction — the post-Cold War model where the US manufactures and allies buy is fracturing. Countries that can produce fast and affordable weapons — whether that's K9 howitzers from South Korea or drones from Ukraine — are winning contracts that would have gone to Lockheed or RTX a decade ago.
sources: Reason, Reuters, Kyiv Post, Politico, Eurasian Times, AFP, Asharq Al-Awsat, Firstpost, UPI, Seoulz, Polish Radio, Drop Site News, RUSI, Lowy Institute, Cato Institute, SBS Australia, SCMP, Business Standard, Moneycontrol, DB Investment & Securities
